collagen.rationale: Hydrolyzed collagen peptides support connective tissue synthesis and joint integrity; morning timing with Vitamin C maximizes cross-linking efficiency.
science_points: Hydrolyzed collagen peptides increase type-II collagen synthesis markers in joint cartilage (Shaw et al., 2017); meta-analysis shows significant reduction in joint pain in active individuals.
Rich in glycine and proline — amino acids largely absent from standard protein sources and essential for collagen triple-helix formation.
10g/day over 12 weeks shown to improve joint function scores in athletes with knee complaints vs. placebo.

probiotics.rationale: Multi-strain probiotic to support microbiome diversity and gut-axis signaling pathways.
science_points: Multiple RCTs show multi-strain probiotics reduce GI symptoms and improve stool consistency; gut microbiome diversity correlates with improved metabolic and mood markers.
Morning dosing with food optimizes survivability of Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium strains through gastric transit.
Gut-brain axis signaling via vagal pathways; several studies show probiotic supplementation reduces self-reported stress and anxiety scores vs. placebo.

electrolytes.rationale: Sodium/potassium/magnesium replenishment to support hydration, nerve conduction, and morning cardiovascular function.
science_points: Sodium losses during training are highly individual; even mild sodium deficit impairs muscle contraction efficiency and cardiovascular output during exercise.
Electrolyte balance is essential for membrane potential maintenance across muscle and nerve cells — fundamental to all electrical signaling in the body.
Morning supplementation front-loads hydration status before training, reducing performance impairment from even mild dehydration (~2% body weight).

l_glutamine.rationale: Primary fuel for intestinal enterocytes; supports gut lining integrity and immune function during high training load.
science_points: Glutamine is the most conditionally essential amino acid during intense exercise — plasma levels drop significantly post-training, correlating with immune suppression markers.
Supplementation at 5g/day shown to reduce intestinal permeability (‘leaky gut’) markers in clinical trials; key for maintaining gut barrier function under metabolic stress.
5g/day restores mucosal secretory IgA levels post-exercise, reducing incidence of upper respiratory tract infections in overtrained athletes.

protein_supplement.rationale: Protein target of 1.6–2.2g/kg requires supplementation; co-ingested with meals to optimize leucine kinetics and minimize time below muscle protein synthesis threshold.
science_points: Meta-analysis of 49 RCTs (Morton et al., 2018) confirms protein supplementation significantly augments lean mass and strength gains in resistance-trained individuals.
Leucine threshold (~3g per serving) maximizes mTORC1-mediated muscle protein synthesis; whole-food meals often fail to reliably hit this threshold.
Protein distribution across 4–6 meals throughout the day optimizes 24-hour net protein balance vs. front- or back-loading.

l_threonate.rationale: The only form of magnesium with demonstrated blood-brain barrier penetration; taken pre-sleep to support synaptic density and GABA-ergic sleep induction.
science_points: MIT-derived compound (Slutsky et al., 2010): L-Threonate chelate enables Mg2+ transport across the blood-brain barrier — unlike oxide, citrate, or glycinate forms which do not meaningfully elevate brain Mg.
Animal studies show 15% increase in synaptic density with long-term supplementation; human RCTs show improved cognitive performance and sleep quality vs. other Mg forms.
Before-bed timing aligns with Mg’s GABA-ergic and NMDA-antagonist mechanisms for sleep induction and slow-wave sleep quality.

apigenin.rationale: Competitive CD38 inhibitor and partial GABA-A agonist; taken pre-sleep for anxiolytic effects and potential NAD+ preservation via CD38 suppression.
science_points: Apigenin binds GABA-A receptors at the benzodiazepine allosteric site — partial agonist mechanism produces anxiolytic and mild sedative effects without full BZD dependence risk or REM suppression.
CD38 is the primary enzyme degrading NAD+ to ADPR; apigenin’s competitive CD38 inhibition may enhance NAD+ bioavailability, synergizing with NAD+ precursor strategies.
Human RCT evidence is limited; evidence base is primarily mechanistic and pharmacological. Part of the Huberman sleep protocol based on early rodent and mechanistic data.

theanine.rationale: Non-sedating anxiolytic that promotes alpha brain wave activity; taken pre-sleep for relaxed transition to sleep without next-morning grogginess.
science_points: Well-replicated RCTs show 200mg L-Theanine increases alpha-band EEG power, reduces perceived stress, and improves subjective sleep quality without sedation or impairment the following day.
Modulates glutamate receptor activity (AMPA, kainate) and promotes GABA release — distinct from benzodiazepine mechanisms, no tolerance or dependence risk at standard doses.
Synergistic with Magnesium L-Threonate for non-pharmacological sleep induction; combination shown superior to either alone for sleep onset latency in small RCTs.

vitamin_d3.rationale: VDR gene variant (Bsm1) reduces receptor binding efficiency, requiring higher-than-standard dosing to maintain adequate serum 25(OH)D levels.
science_points: VDR Bsm1 polymorphism is associated with reduced Vitamin D receptor transcriptional activity, necessitating higher circulating levels for equivalent downstream effect.
Serum 25(OH)D above 40 ng/mL is associated with improved immune function, mood regulation, and musculoskeletal health in RCTs.
At northern latitudes, endogenous synthesis is insufficient October–April regardless of sun exposure.

creatine.rationale: Most well-studied ergogenic aid in existence — consistent strength and power output gains, with emerging cognitive benefit data.
science_points: Increases phosphocreatine stores in muscle, accelerating ATP resynthesis during high-intensity efforts; meta-analyses show ~8% strength gain over placebo.
Creatine supplementation increases brain creatine concentrations and is associated with improved working memory and processing speed in low-meat-intake populations.
5g/day is sufficient for maintenance after a loading phase; no kidney safety concerns in healthy individuals at this dose.

omega3.rationale: FADS2 rs1535 variant impairs conversion of ALA to EPA/DHA, making pre-formed fish oil essential rather than optional for anti-inflammatory benefit.
science_points: EPA and DHA are precursors to resolvins and protectins — pro-resolving lipid mediators that actively terminate inflammatory cascades rather than merely inhibiting initiation.
High-dose Omega-3 (2–4g/day EPA+DHA) significantly reduces triglycerides; cardiovascular mortality reduction seen in REDUCE-IT at 4g/day icosapentaenoic acid.
FADS2 rs1535 T-allele carriers show substantially lower ALA conversion; pre-formed EPA/DHA is the only reliable dietary source for this genotype.

tongkat_ali.rationale: Testing whether Eurycoma longifolia supplementation produces measurable improvement in recovery metrics and WHOOP strain-to-recovery ratio.
science_points: Small RCTs (n=76, 300mg/day for 12 weeks) show statistically significant increases in free testosterone in late-middle-aged men; effect size modest.
Proposed mechanism: quassinoids inhibit SHBG binding, increasing bioavailable testosterone fraction rather than stimulating production directly.
Evidence base is thin — most trials are industry-funded, small, and short-duration; replication in independent labs is limited.

nmn.rationale: Testing NAD+ precursor supplementation as a strategy for maintaining mitochondrial function and metabolic flexibility as a longevity lever.
science_points: NAD+ declines ~50% between ages 40 and 60; NMN is efficiently converted to NAD+ in humans (Yoshino et al., 2021 — confirmed bioavailability and blood NAD+ elevation at 250mg/day).
Animal studies show substantial metabolic and longevity effects; human RCT data is promising but limited — largest trial (n=80) showed improved muscle insulin sensitivity.
Optimal dose, long-term safety profile, and disease-relevant outcomes in humans remain understudied; this is an informed speculative bet.

coq10.rationale: Mitochondrial electron transport chain cofactor; ubiquinol form selected for superior bioavailability vs. ubiquinone, particularly important given past statin use history.
science_points: Statins inhibit the mevalonate pathway, reducing endogenous CoQ10 synthesis by 40–50%; supplementation at 100–200mg/day restores serum levels to pre-statin baseline.
CoQ10 is the mobile electron carrier between Complex I/II and Complex III in the mitochondrial electron transport chain — essential for ATP synthesis efficiency.
Meta-analysis of 13 RCTs (Mortensen et al.) shows 200mg ubiquinol produces plasma CoQ10 concentrations approximately twice that of equivalent ubiquinone dose.

berberine.rationale: Testing CGM-measurable postprandial glucose attenuation, particularly for high-carbohydrate meals where my response tends to spike above 140 mg/dL.
science_points: AMPK activator — mimics the downstream effects of metformin by increasing insulin sensitivity and reducing hepatic glucose output; several RCTs show HbA1c reduction comparable to metformin in T2D populations.
Inhibits intestinal alpha-glucosidase, slowing carbohydrate absorption and blunting postprandial glucose spikes; effect is dose- and timing-dependent (take 20–30 min before eating).
Short-term GI discomfort is common at doses above 500mg; bioavailability is low (~5%) but sufficient for AMPK activation at standard doses.

magnesium_glycinate.rationale: Magnesium is a cofactor in 300+ enzymatic reactions; glycinate chelate selected for superior bioavailability and minimal laxative effect compared to oxide or citrate forms.
science_points: Magnesium is required for NMDA receptor regulation and GABA-ergic signalling — both pathways involved in sleep induction and slow-wave sleep quality.
Meta-analysis of 8 RCTs shows magnesium supplementation significantly reduces subjective insomnia severity and improves sleep efficiency in adults with poor sleep quality.
Glycinate chelate bypasses intestinal magnesium transporters that become saturated at high doses, achieving higher serum levels with less GI disruption than inorganic salts.

zinc.rationale: Zinc metabolism SNP reduces intestinal zinc absorption efficiency, requiring supplementation to maintain adequate serum zinc status for immune and hormonal function.
science_points: Zinc is a cofactor for over 300 enzymes and is essential for testosterone biosynthesis — clinical zinc deficiency is associated with hypogonadism that reverses with supplementation.
SLC39A4 (ZIP4) and SLC30A series transporter variants affect zinc homeostasis; impaired absorption variants may double dietary requirement to maintain serum adequacy.
Taken in the evening due to competitive absorption with copper (maintained separately at breakfast via diet); evening zinc avoids meal-time mineral interference.

ashwagandha.rationale: Cortisol modulation and stress-resilience support; KSM-66 extract selected as the most clinically studied form with the largest body of human RCT evidence.
science_points: Withania somnifera root extract at 600mg/day (KSM-66) reduces serum cortisol by ~25–28% vs. placebo in chronically stressed adults across multiple double-blind RCTs.
Randomized trial (Wankhede et al., 2015) showed significant improvements in muscle recovery, testosterone, and upper/lower body strength vs. placebo over 8 weeks of resistance training.
Evening dosing aligns with the goal of attenuating late-day cortisol elevation that impairs sleep onset; half-life is approximately 6–8 hours.
